inbound.subject: Starting over after a layoff
inbound.body: Hi coach, I want to get back to running after a messy few months. My big goal is to get healthy enough to race a half marathon again this spring. Right now I mostly need help rebuilding without doing something stupid.

outbound.subject: Re: Starting over after a layoff
outbound.text:
Thanks — rough few months, and I hear you: steady, careful rebuild to get back to a half without doing anything stupid.
One quick thing I need to start your plan: what’s your weekly time availability for training? Tell me either days per week you can train and typical session length, or a rough total weekly hours. Examples: “3 days — 40–60 min per run” or “5–6 hours/week.”
Once I have that, I’ll draft a gradual, setback-focused rebuilding plan for the coming weeks and send the first phase you can start with. If there’s anything urgent (injury, pain, or medical restrictions), mention that when you reply.

inbound.subject: Profile details and target race
inbound.body: Here is the missing context more directly. Primary goal: run a half marathon on 2026-05-17 and finish feeling strong. Time availability: 4 sessions per week and about 4 to 5 hours total. Experience level: intermediate. Constraints: mild Achilles tightness, busy work mornings on Tuesdays, and I prefer structured guidance.

outbound.subject: Re: Profile details and target race
outbound.text:
Hi there,
You’re aiming to run a half marathon on 2026-05-17 and want a steady, cautious rebuild. With four sessions per week and about 4–5 hours total, mild Achilles tightness, and Tuesday mornings busy, we’ll keep load controlled and structure this week around easy, progressive work.
Today: keep it easy and monitor pain.
This week’s plan (main_build, build phase, risk yellow)
Session 1 — easy_aerobic
- 60–75 minutes total at an easy, conversational pace
- 10-minute warm-up, 0–10 minutes of light mobility as needed, then the run, and 5–10 minute cooldown
- After-run, 5–10 minutes of Achilles/calves mobility if needed
- Cue: cadence light, posture tall
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade to easy and do not push through
Session 2 — mobility
- 20–30 minutes of focused ankle, calf, and hip mobility
- Keep effort light; no high-load work
Session 3 — strength
- 25–40 minutes
- Exercises: glute bridges or hip thrusts, single-leg balance or split squats, calf raises with controlled tempo, core work (dead bug or side plank)
- 2–3 sets of 8–12 reps per exercise; slow, controlled tempo
- Prioritize form and pain-free movement
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, adjust accordingly
Session 4 — easy_aerobic
- 60–75 minutes easy run, same structure as Session 1
- Cadence and posture cues apply; keep it comfortable
Weekly priorities
- Hit the four sessions with controlled effort; avoid extra load
- Support the work with good sleep and simple recovery habits (hydration, light mobility, easy walks if needed)
- Maintain a tall posture and light cadence to protect the Achilles
Safety note
- No hard sessions if risk is elevated; downgrade any session if pain or inflammation increases
If you need to fit the days around your Tuesday schedule, you can arrange sessions on Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and either Saturday or Sunday while keeping the same four-session balance.
Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Availability update for this week
inbound.body: Availability update for this week. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate feels green. I can run Monday, Wednesday, Friday, and Sunday. Week is not chaotic. Schedule variability is medium because work may move one session by a few hours.

outbound.subject: Re: Availability update for this week
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Thanks for the availability update. With four sessions this week and a mild Achilles tightness (pain 2/10), we keep things controlled and focused on staying healthy while rebuilding base fitness for the May half.
This week's plan (Main Base, four sessions):
- Monday — easy_aerobic: 60–75 minutes. Keep effort easy, cadence light, posture tall. After run: calves/Achilles mobility and simple recovery work.
- Wednesday — tempo: steady, controlled effort at a moderate pace. Include a warm-up and cool-down. If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade to easy.
- Friday — easy_aerobic: 60–75 minutes. Same cues as Monday; finish with Achilles/calves mobility.
- Sunday — strength: 25–40 minutes; focus on controlled movements with good form; avoid high impact on the Achilles.
Key safety notes:
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy and do not make up missed intensity later.
- Keep cadence light and posture tall; support the work with steady sleep and simple recovery habits.
Logistics and adjustments:
- If a session needs to shift by a few hours due to work, keep the same order and intensity.
- Continue to monitor Achilles after each run and report any increase in pain or new issues so we can adjust quickly.
Optional sign-off: Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: First check-in after an easy run
inbound.body: Training check-in. Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is green. I did a run for 45m and 6 km yesterday. Felt good, energy ok, little sore, slept well. Missed sessions count: 0. I want to keep building carefully.

outbound.subject: Re: First check-in after an easy run
outbound.text:
Hi there,
Nice work on the check‑in. A 2/10 pain with energy feeling okay is a signal to stay steady and cautious this week. We’ll run the updated conservative plan aligned to your Mon/Wed/Fri/Sun availability and your mild Achilles sensitivity.
This week’s plan (base phase, main_base):
- Monday — easy aerobic 60–75 minutes. Easy effort, cadence light, posture tall. After the run: calf/achilles mobility and simple recovery habits.
- Wednesday — controlled tempo with warm-up and cool-down. Moderate effort; downgrade to easy immediately if pain or fatigue rises.
- Friday — easy aerobic 60–75 minutes. Same cues as Monday.
- Sunday — strength 25–40 minutes. Controlled movements; avoid high Achilles impact.
Key cues for all sessions: keep cadence light, posture tall, and support the work with sleep and simple recovery habits. If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy and do not try to make up missed intensity later in the week.
Event is 2026-05-17, and the plan stays at a conservative pace to rebuild running fitness without pushing the Achilles beyond a tolerable limit. You’ve reported no missed sessions, and risk remains green.
If you want, you can share after the week how it went. Let me know how it goes.
Coach

Event is 2026-05-17, and the plan stays at a conservative pace to rebuild running fitness without pushing the Achilles beyond a tolerable limit. You’ve reported no missed sessions, and risk remains green.

Coach

inbound.subject: How easy should easy feel?
inbound.body: Quick question: on these comeback runs, how easy should easy actually feel? I'm trying not to chase pace, but I also don't want to jog so slowly that the mechanics get weird.

outbound.subject: Re: How easy should easy feel?
outbound.text:
Good question — short answer: easy should feel easy and conversational, but not so slow that you start to shuffle.
Practical cues to hit on these comeback runs:
- Effort: aim for an RPE around 2–3 out of 10 (comfortable, sustainable for the whole session). You should be able to speak in full sentences without gasping. Don’t chase pace—some days will be slower and that’s fine. 
- Mechanics: keep a natural running form — upright posture, slight forward lean, quick-ish turnover, feet landing under your hips. If you notice shuffling, overstriding, or long ground contact, pick the pace up a little until form feels normal. 
- Simple drill to protect form: after 10–15 minutes easy warm-up, do 4×20–30s gentle pickups (not sprints) with full recovery between. Then return to easy. They help maintain cadence without loading the Achilles too much. 
- Achilles safety rule: if pain rises noticeably (more than ~2 points from baseline or any sharp/increasing pain), immediately back off to walk or stop, shorten the session, and do the mobility/calf routine. If pain stays low and steady, continue as planned. 
Bottom line: prioritize breathing and form over a target pace. Keep it relaxed, use a few short pickups to remind your legs how to run, and protect the Achilles by downgrading if pain increases. Let me know how the next easy run feels.

inbound.subject: Here are the details you probably need
inbound.body: More detail for the adjustment: event date is 2026-05-17, days available this week are 3, pain score is still 2 out of 10, risk candidate feels yellow because work stress is high, week is chaotic true, stress score 8, sleep score 5.

outbound.subject: Re: Here are the details you probably need
outbound.text:
Thanks — got the update. With the week chaotic, low sleep (5/10) and stress high, we’ll keep this simple and controlled: easy work only, monitor the Achilles closely, and avoid any hard efforts.
Plan for your three available days this week (use them as best fits your schedule, spacing them so you have at least one full recovery day between harder sessions):
1) Easy aerobic run — 40–60 minutes
- Effort: conversational, RPE ~3/10. Keep cadence steady and posture tall. Warm up 5–10 min easy, cool down 5–10 min. After the run do 5–10 minutes of calf/Achilles mobility.
2) Strength — 25–35 minutes
- Controlled single-leg and posterior-chain focus: single-leg RDLs, step-ups, glute bridges, supported calf raises, plus 2 short core sets. 2–3 sets, moderate load, slow tempo. Avoid heavy eccentric calf loading or plyometrics.
- Stop or back off if Achilles pain increases beyond baseline (~2/10).
3) Skills + short run — 25–35 minutes total
- 10 min easy warm-up, 8–12 min of form drills (high knees, A-skips, bounding drills at low amplitude), then 3–6 short pickups 15s at relaxed effort only (skip pickups if any twinge). Finish with an easy cooldown and mobility.
Rules to follow this week
- No hard sessions while risk is yellow — keep everything easy and technically tidy.
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy walking/cross-train or cancel. Do not try to make up missed intensity later.
- Keep execution controlled; focus on form and recovery rather than volume.
Simple recovery priorities (because sleep/stress are low):
- Aim for short naps or earlier bedtime where possible, prioritize 1 additional hour of sleep if you can.
- Post-session: small protein-rich snack and hydrate. 5–10 minutes mobility for calves/Achilles after runs.
- If stress is spiking, pick the lower end of the durations above and treat the session as a movement day.
Today: keep it easy and monitor pain closely (follow the above if-then rules).
Let me know how the Achilles and energy feel after your first session and I’ll adjust next week as needed.

inbound.subject: Tune-up effort felt controlled
inbound.body: Small milestone: I did a tune-up 5k effort in 24:50 and it felt controlled. I added a 20m cool-down jog after. No big pain spike, just normal post-workout heaviness, and sleep was good.

outbound.subject: Re: Tune-up effort felt controlled
outbound.text:
Rough week — makes sense to keep this simple. Nice work on the tune-up. A controlled 24:50 for the 5k with a short cooldown and solid sleep is a good sign for moving forward in a yellow-risk week.
To lock in adjustments for next week, please reply with these three items:
- After the first session, did you notice any Achilles pain or changes in your overall energy? If helpful, share pain on a 0–10 scale and energy on a 1–10 scale.
- Which three days and times will you commit to this week’s three easy sessions? (We’ll keep at least one full recovery day between efforts.)
- At week’s end, how did the sessions feel? Do you want any changes to timing or duration for next week?
If sharp pain or fatigue spikes, report it right away so we can adjust. Let me know how it goes.

inbound.subject: Travel details
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 3. Pain score: 2 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow because I am traveling for work. Week is chaotic true. Equipment access is treadmill and gym, no track. I can do 40m treadmill runs and one short strength session.

outbound.subject: Re: Travel details
outbound.text:
Hi there,
This week is hectic—let’s keep it simple and safe. Given travel, limited time, and access to a treadmill and gym, here are three easy sessions that fit your window. We stay on an easy-only pace while risk remains yellow and I’ll adjust if anything changes.
Session plan
- Session 1 (easy aerobic): 40–60 minutes on the treadmill. 5–10 minute warm-up, 30–45 minutes at a relaxed effort (RPE ~3), 5–10 minute cool-down. Finish with 5–10 minutes of calf/ankle mobility post-run.
- Session 2 (strength): 25–35 minutes. Controlled single-leg and posterior-chain work with moderate load. Avoid heavy eccentric calf loading or plyometrics. If Achilles pain rises, back off and stop.
- Session 3 (skills + short run): 25–35 minutes. Form drills plus 3–6 relaxed pickups. If any twinge occurs, skip the pickups and keep it easy. Include a 5 minute warm-up and a 5–10 minute easy jog to finish.
Safety and execution
- No hard sessions while risk is yellow.
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade the session to easy.
- Do not make up missed intensity later in the week.
Recovery and hygiene
- Prioritize sleep, a small protein snack post-session, hydration, and calf/ankle mobility work.
Plan review and next steps
- I’ll review your post-session pain and energy after the first session and adjust next week’s plan as needed.
If you want any timing tweaks within your three-session window, I can accommodate. Let me know how it goes.
Best,
Coach

inbound.subject: Details for the family-chaos week
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 2. Pain score: 3 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow. Family stuff blew up this week. I only got in a 30m run. Felt low energy, sore, poor sleep. Stress score is 9. I am worried about losing momentum.

outbound.subject: Re: Details for the family-chaos week
outbound.text:
I hear you — family chaos, very high stress and poor sleep. Keep this week deliberately simple: two controlled sessions that protect the body and preserve momentum without adding load.
Plan for the week (2 sessions):
Session A — Easy aerobic (35–45 min treadmill)
- 5–10 min very easy warm‑up, then 25–30 min relaxed steady effort (RPE ~3), 5–10 min cool‑down.
- Focus on light cadence, tall posture, relaxed breathing. Keep effort conversational. If energy is low, shorten to 20–25 min rather than pushing pace.
- Finish with 5–10 min calf/ankle mobility and gentle hamstring/glute activation.
Session B — Strength + short skills run (30–40 min total)
- 20–30 min strength: controlled single‑leg RDLs, glute bridges, split squats, lateral band work, core. Use moderate load, slow control; avoid heavy eccentric calf loading and any plyometrics.
- Follow with 8–12 min easy run or drills: warm drills (A skips, high knees) and 2–3 relaxed pickups only if there’s zero twinge. Skip pickups if any increase in pain.
Execution priorities (do these every session):
- No hard sessions while risk is elevated (YELLOW). Keep everything easy and controlled.
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade immediately to walking or cancel the session. Do not try to make up missed intensity later in the week.
- Monitor the Achilles/calf area closely — stop if pain increases from baseline or feels inflamed.
- Recovery basics: prioritize sleep, have a small protein snack after sessions, hydrate, and do brief mobility.
Safety trigger (explicit): if pain increases notably (for example from 3/10 up to ~5/10 or you notice swelling/redness), stop training and rest — downgrade all sessions to easy walking until pain settles.
This plan keeps you moving without forcing load through a stressful week. It won’t erase training, it protects it — and that’s the best way to keep momentum. Let me know how session A goes (pain and overall energy) and I’ll adjust next week from there.
outbound.html:
I hear you — family chaos, very high stress and poor sleep. Keep this week deliberately simple: two controlled sessions that protect the body and preserve momentum without adding load.

Plan for the week (2 sessions):

Session A — Easy aerobic (35–45 min treadmill)
- 5–10 min very easy warm‑up, then 25–30 min relaxed steady effort (RPE ~3), 5–10 min cool‑down.
- Focus on light cadence, tall posture, relaxed breathing. Keep effort conversational. If energy is low, shorten to 20–25 min rather than pushing pace.
- Finish with 5–10 min calf/ankle mobility and gentle hamstring/glute activation.

Session B — Strength + short skills run (30–40 min total)
- 20–30 min strength: controlled single‑leg RDLs, glute bridges, split squats, lateral band work, core. Use moderate load, slow control; avoid heavy eccentric calf loading and any plyometrics.
- Follow with 8–12 min easy run or drills: warm drills (A skips, high knees) and 2–3 relaxed pickups only if there’s zero twinge. Skip pickups if any increase in pain.

Execution priorities (do these every session):
- No hard sessions while risk is elevated (YELLOW). Keep everything easy and controlled.
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully, downgrade immediately to walking or cancel the session. Do not try to make up missed intensity later in the week.
- Monitor the Achilles/calf area closely — stop if pain increases from baseline or feels inflamed.
- Recovery basics: prioritize sleep, have a small protein snack after sessions, hydrate, and do brief mobility.

Safety trigger (explicit): if pain increases notably (for example from 3/10 up to ~5/10 or you notice swelling/redness), stop training and rest — downgrade all sessions to easy walking until pain settles.

This plan keeps you moving without forcing load through a stressful week. It won’t erase training, it protects it — and that’s the best way to keep momentum. Let me know how session A goes (pain and overall energy) and I’ll adjust next week from there.

inbound.body: Better week. I ran three times: 35m easy, 50m steady, and 80m today for about 9 miles. Felt good, slept well, and the Achilles feels quiet again. Same four-day rhythm still seems like the sweet spot.

outbound.subject: Re: Back on track
outbound.text:
Nice to hear you’re back on track and that the Achilles is quiet. Given the week’s high stress and the two-session window, we’ll keep to the two easy sessions and avoid any hard loads.
To lock in the week, please confirm:
1) The two days/times you’ll run Session A (easy aerobic 35–45 minutes on treadmill with 5–10 min warm-up and 5–10 min mobility) and Session B (30–40 minutes total combining 20–30 minutes controlled strength with 8–12 minutes easy drills or a short jog), with at least one full recovery day between them.

If pain rises notably or swelling appears, stop and adjust per safety rules.
Thanks—looking forward to your update.

inbound.subject: Harder session left me cooked
inbound.body: Yesterday I did a harder session: total run 55m with 6 x 3 minutes moderate-hard. Today I'm pretty cooked, sleep was poor, and soreness is definitely up. I don't think anything is injured, but it feels like I pushed the edge.

outbound.subject: Re: Harder session left me cooked
outbound.text:
Rough week — makes sense to keep this simple.
Two easy, controlled sessions this week to protect tissue and keep momentum toward your May race:
Session A — Easy aerobic: 35–45 minutes on the treadmill
- Structure: 5–10 min warm-up, 25–30 min relaxed pace (RPE ~3), 5–10 min cool-down
- Afterward: 5–10 min calf/ankle mobility
- Focus: steady, comfortable effort; cadence light, posture tall
Session B — Strength + short skills/run: 30–40 minutes total
- 20–30 min controlled strength (avoid heavy eccentric calf loading; single-leg and posterior-chain work)
- 8–12 min easy drills or short run; skip pickups if any twinge
Delivery rules and safety
- No hard sessions while risk is yellow
- If pain or fatigue rises notably, downgrade to easy
- Do not make up missed intensity later in the week
- Stop if pain increases notably (5/10) or swelling/redness appears
Recovery and daily habits
- Prioritize sleep, hydration, and a small protein snack post-session
Logistics
- Equipment available: treadmill and gym; no track this week
- Schedule: two sessions this week, with at least one recovery day between them
Optional note: Let me know how Session A goes and we will reassess next week.
You have this.

inbound.subject: Numbers from the hard session
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 4. Pain score: 5 out of 10. Risk candidate feels yellow. Intervals yesterday: run 55m total with 6 x 3 minutes moderate-hard. Felt exhausted after, soreness high, sleep poor. Pain affects form false. Pain worsening false. No swelling.

outbound.subject: Re: Numbers from the hard session
outbound.text:
Noted — you pushed a harder 55‑minute session yesterday and you’re feeling cooked: pain 5/10, high soreness, poor sleep, and low energy. Good call to step back; we’ll keep this week deliberately simple and protective.
Top priority: protect the achilles and your sleep/energy. No hard sessions while risk is yellow. Follow the minimum effective dose so you preserve consistency without inflaming the tissue.
This week (2 controlled sessions total — use two of your available 4 days, with at least one full recovery day between them):
Session A — Easy aerobic (35–45 min, treadmill)
- Structure: 5–10 min easy warm‑up, 25–30 min continuous relaxed running at RPE ~3 (conversational), 5–10 min easy cool‑down. 
- Cues: light cadence, posture tall, short ground contact. Keep effort honest and conservative — slow down if breathing edges up. 
- Finish with 5–10 min calf/ankle mobility and gentle calf/soleus stretching (pain‑free range).
Session B — Strength + short skills/run (30–40 min total)
- Strength 20–30 min: controlled, moderate loads; focus on glutes, hips, single‑leg stability, core. Avoid heavy eccentric calf loading (no heavy heel drops, no plyometrics). Example: goblet squats, single‑leg RDL with light weight, lateral band walks, step‑ups, plank progressions. 
- Follow with 8–12 min very easy drills or an easy jog if feeling fine — skip any pickups or faster reps.
Daily rules and priorities
- Energy is the limiting signal: if you feel worse during a session, cut it short rather than forcing completion. 
- If you rebound tomorrow, resume cautiously — do not try to “make up” hard work later. 
- Focus recovery: prioritize sleep, a small protein snack after sessions, hydration, and downshifting stress in the evening. 
- Safety stop: if pain increases notably above baseline (~>5/10), or you see swelling/redness, stop immediately and rest/seek assessment. No hard sessions while risk remains yellow.
Keep the execution conservative — shorter, easier, and consistent this week is the win. Let me know briefly after Session A how you feel (energy and pain) and we’ll set the next week from there.

inbound.subject: Three-day week details
inbound.body: Event date: 2026-05-17. Days available: 3. Pain score: 3 out of 10. Risk candidate is yellow. Next week I only have three training days. Week is chaotic false, but volume has to come down. Please simplify the week and protect the key session.

outbound.subject: Re: Three-day week details
outbound.text:
Got it — three training days, lower volume, pain ~3/10. We'll keep the week simple, protect the key aerobic session, and stay conservative while you recover.
Today: easy only — keep movement very relaxed and watch the Achilles. Stop or switch to walking if pain increases.
Planned week (three controlled sessions)
1) Key session — Easy aerobic (35–45 minutes, treadmill)
- 5–10 min very easy warm-up
- 25–30 min continuous relaxed run, RPE ~3 (conversational)
- 5–10 min cool-down + 5–10 min calf/ankle mobility
- Cues: light, even cadence; short ground contact; keep breathing steady. No pickups or faster efforts.
2) Strength + skills (30–35 minutes, gym + short easy drills)
- 20–25 min controlled strength work: focus on glutes, hips, core and single‑leg stability. Avoid heavy eccentric calf loading and any plyometrics.
- Examples: goblet squats / Romanian deadlifts (moderate load), single‑leg balance/step‑downs (controlled), glute bridges, anti‑rotation core work — 2 sets of 8–12 reps where appropriate.
- Finish with 8–10 min very easy drills or an easy jog; skip pickups if anything twinges.
3) Easy aerobic recovery (20–35 minutes)
- Keep it shorter and very easy, RPE 2–3. Use this as active recovery to keep the legs moving without adding load.
How to place the sessions
- Leave at least one full recovery day between efforts (e.g., session, rest, session, rest, session). If that spacing isn’t possible, prioritize keeping the key aerobic session fully protected.
Execution priorities and safety
- Keep effort smooth and technically tidy; do not chase volume this week.
- If pain or fatigue rises meaningfully during a session, immediately downgrade to easy walking or stop. If pain reaches >~5/10 or you see swelling/redness, stop and seek assessment.
- Do not try to make up missed intensity later in the week.
- Support sessions with steady sleep, good hydration, and a small post‑session protein snack.
Short checklist for each session
- Pre: how does Achilles feel? If noticeably worse, make the session shorter/easier or skip.
- During: stay at assigned RPE; avoid bursts of speed.
- Post: 5–10 min mobility for calf/ankle and note pain level.
Keep it simple this week — protect the key aerobic session and prioritize recovery. Monitor pain closely and tell me how the key session feels (pre/post pain and overall energy) so I can adjust next week.

outbound.subject: Re: Race confidence check
outbound.text:
Thanks — quick clarifying items so I can give a clear two‑week focus that protects the Achilles:
1) For each of the next two weeks, how many training days can you actually commit to: your usual 4 days, or only 3 days each week? 
2) Right now, what is your Achilles pain on a 0–10 scale at rest and immediately after your last run (give both numbers)?
Once you confirm those two points I’ll send a focused, protected plan and the single biggest priority for the next two weeks.
